packs.name: Python
packs.description: Detects destructive operations via Python inline commands

destructive_patterns.name: shutil-rmtree
destructive_patterns.reason: Python shutil.rmtree() recursively deletes directory trees.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
shutil.rmtree() is Python's equivalent of rm -rf. It recursively deletes an entire directory tree without confirmation.

This is irreversible and bypasses the trash.

destructive_patterns.name: os-remove
destructive_patterns.explanation: os.remove() and os.unlink() permanently delete a single file. The deletion is immediate and irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: os-rmdir
destructive_patterns.explanation: os.rmdir() removes a single empty directory. os.removedirs() removes directories recursively (only empty ones, but can remove a chain).

destructive_patterns.name: pathlib-rmtree
destructive_patterns.reason: Python pathlib rmdir/unlink permanently deletes files or directories.
destructive_patterns.explanation: pathlib.Path.unlink() deletes a file and pathlib.Path.rmdir() removes an empty directory. Both are irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: subprocess-destructive
destructive_patterns.reason: Python subprocess executing commands can be destructive.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
subprocess.run(), subprocess.call(), subprocess.Popen(), and related functions execute arbitrary shell commands. When used with destructive commands like rm, they bypass Python's file API safety.

Review the command being executed carefully before allowing.

destructive_patterns.name: open-truncate
destructive_patterns.explanation: Opening a file and calling truncate() erases its contents. This is irreversible and can destroy critical system files or configuration data.

packs.name: Node.js
packs.description: Detects destructive operations via Node.js inline commands

destructive_patterns.name: fs-rmsync
destructive_patterns.reason: Node.js fs.rmSync()/rmdirSync() recursively deletes files and directories.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
fs.rmSync() with {recursive: true} is Node's equivalent of rm -rf. fs.rmdirSync() with {recursive: true} is the older API with the same effect.

Both are irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: fs-unlinksync
destructive_patterns.explanation: fs.unlinkSync() permanently deletes a file. The deletion is immediate and irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: fs-rm-async
destructive_patterns.reason: Node.js fs.rm()/fs.promises.rm() asynchronously deletes files and directories.
destructive_patterns.explanation: The async rm/rmdir functions with {recursive: true} delete entire directory trees. Async deletion can be harder to interrupt once started.

destructive_patterns.name: fs-writefilesync
destructive_patterns.reason: Node.js fs.writeFileSync() overwrites file contents, potentially destroying data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
fs.writeFileSync() overwrites the entire contents of a file. When used on system files like /etc/passwd or configuration files, it can destroy critical data and break system functionality.

Review the target file path carefully before allowing.

packs.name: Ruby
packs.description: Detects destructive operations via Ruby inline commands

destructive_patterns.name: fileutils-rm-rf
destructive_patterns.reason: Ruby FileUtils.rm_rf recursively deletes directory trees.
destructive_patterns.explanation: FileUtils.rm_rf is Ruby's equivalent of rm -rf. It recursively deletes an entire directory tree without confirmation. Irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: fileutils-rm
destructive_patterns.explanation: FileUtils.rm deletes one or more files permanently. With force: true, it suppresses errors for missing files.

destructive_patterns.name: file-delete
destructive_patterns.explanation: File.delete (alias File.unlink) permanently removes one or more files.

destructive_patterns.name: dir-rmdir
destructive_patterns.explanation: Dir.rmdir removes an empty directory. Combined with recursive file deletion, it can remove entire directory trees.

packs.name: Perl
packs.description: Detects destructive operations via Perl inline commands

destructive_patterns.name: remove-tree
destructive_patterns.reason: Perl remove_tree()/rmtree() recursively deletes directory trees.
destructive_patterns.explanation: File::Path::remove_tree (and its alias rmtree) is Perl's equivalent of rm -rf. It recursively deletes an entire directory tree. Irreversible.

destructive_patterns.name: unlink
destructive_patterns.explanation: unlink() permanently deletes one or more files. This is a core Perl function with no undo mechanism.

destructive_patterns.name: rmdir
destructive_patterns.explanation: rmdir() removes an empty directory. Often used in combination with file deletion to remove directory trees.
